name: Procurement Intervention
consequences: Influencing AltID's procurement requirements to mandate platform-neutral access clauses directly alters the technical architecture of future identity systems. However, this risks delaying implementation if public-sector stakeholders prioritize vendor familiarity over compliance, creating a gap in immediate fallback capability.
options: Embed platform-neutrality clauses in AltID's procurement contracts through direct negotiation with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen
Leverage EU eIDAS2 compliance mandates to require fallback authentication pathways in all public-sector identity procurements
Create a tiered certification process for identity providers that prioritizes supplier diversity and fallback capability
review: Procurement intervention risks institutional delay if stakeholders prioritize vendor familiarity, leaving a gap in immediate fallback capability without parallel technical demonstrations.

name: Technical Demonstration
consequences: Developing non-production demonstrators proves the feasibility of platform-neutral authentication models but creates a dependency on controlled environments that may not reflect real-world deployment challenges. This risks overestimating adoption potential if user experience trade-offs are not adequately addressed.
options: Build a FIDO2/WebAuthn-based demonstrator using GrapheneOS to bypass platform-specific integrity checks
Create a browser-based authentication flow that bypasses mobile OS app-store dependencies through decentralized identity protocols
Develop a hardware-token fallback mechanism integrated with existing Danish public-sector identity providers
review: Technical demonstrations may overstate adoption potential if user experience trade-offs are not addressed, leaving a gap in understanding real-world usability challenges.

name: Regulatory Framing
consequences: Reframing the issue as a resilience and continuity risk rather than a privacy concern shifts policy priorities but may dilute attention on democratic accountability and data protection implications. This risks creating regulatory gaps in oversight of alternative authentication models.
options: Position platform neutrality as a critical infrastructure resilience requirement in national cybersecurity strategies
Advocate for updated NSIS standards that explicitly address fallback authentication in high-assurance scenarios
Integrate supplier concentration risk assessments into existing public-sector procurement audit frameworks
review: Resilience framing may dilute attention on data protection implications, leaving a gap in oversight of alternative authentication models' privacy risks.

name: Coalition Building
consequences: Mobilizing cross-sector stakeholders strengthens advocacy but creates coordination challenges that may slow decision-making. This risks fragmenting influence if coalition members have conflicting priorities regarding technical implementation or policy scope.
options: Form a multi-stakeholder advisory board including civil society, academia, and public-sector IT leaders to co-design fallback requirements
Partner with European digital rights organizations to align Danish advocacy with broader interoperability initiatives
Establish a technical working group with former MitID stakeholders to ensure continuity with existing infrastructure
review: Coalition building risks fragmentation if stakeholder priorities conflict, leaving a gap in unified technical implementation guidance.

name: EU Standards Engagement
consequences: Influencing European Digital Identity Wallet frameworks creates broader impact but depends on slow EU regulatory processes. This risks delaying national implementation if European standards diverge from Danish requirements or face political resistance.
options: Propose platform-neutral access requirements in European Digital Identity Wallet technical working groups
Align Danish fallback authentication concepts with upcoming eIDAS3 regulatory updates through targeted consultations
Leverage EU Horizon funding programs to support research on decentralized identity resilience models
review: EU engagement risks delayed national implementation if European standards diverge, leaving a gap in immediate domestic policy traction.

name: Institutional Capacity Building
consequences: Strengthening public-sector expertise in alternative authentication models enables long-term sustainability but requires sustained investment. This risks creating dependency on project-specific knowledge if institutional learning mechanisms are not embedded.
options: Develop a training program for public-sector IT leaders on platform-neutral identity architectures and fallback strategies
Create a shared technical repository for fallback authentication use cases and compliance checklists
Establish a permanent advisory panel on digital identity resilience within Digitaliseringsstyrelsen
review: Capacity building risks knowledge dependency if institutional learning mechanisms are not embedded, leaving a gap in long-term technical oversight.

name: Alternative Authentication Ecosystems
consequences: Creating alternative authentication ecosystems reduces reliance on foreign platforms but risks fragmentation if standards are not universally adopted. This approach could accelerate local innovation but may face resistance from stakeholders invested in existing vendor relationships.
options: Develop open-source authentication protocols compatible with multiple platforms
Partner with local fintechs to create alternative verification channels
Implement hardware-based token systems for high-security access
review: Prioritizing open-source protocols may slow adoption due to integration challenges, leaving a gap in immediate deployment readiness.

name: Regulatory Diversification
consequences: Pushing for regulations that mandate alternative authentication methods could force platform neutrality but might trigger legal challenges from dominant vendors. This lever risks delaying policy adoption if stakeholders perceive it as anti-competitive.
options: Draft regulatory frameworks requiring multi-provider authentication options
Engage EU technical working groups to align Danish standards with broader interoperability goals
Create compliance benchmarks for fallback authentication pathways
review: Mandating alternative methods could provoke litigation from platform providers, leaving a gap in legal preparedness for counterarguments.

name: Public-Private Resilience Partnerships
consequences: Forming collaborations between public agencies and private sector actors could pool resources for alternative solutions but may introduce conflicts of interest. This lever risks diluting focus on sovereignty if private partners prioritize commercial gains over public infrastructure goals.
options: Establish joint task forces to co-develop platform-neutral authentication pilots
Co-fund research initiatives with local tech firms to explore alternative trust models
Create shared risk-mitigation protocols for fallback authentication systems
review: Shared funding structures may prioritize commercial viability over public-sector resilience, leaving a gap in pure sovereignty-focused outcomes.

name: Cross-Platform Interoperability Protocols
consequences: Designing protocols that enable cross-platform authentication could reduce vendor lock-in but requires consensus among competing stakeholders. This lever risks technical stagnation if alignment proves unattainable across diverse ecosystem requirements.
options: Develop open APIs for interoperable authentication across iOS, Android, and Linux environments
Test interoperability frameworks with non-Danish public-sector identity providers
Create certification processes for third-party authentication service providers
review: Achieving cross-platform consensus may delay implementation, leaving a gap in immediate fallback capability during transitional periods.

name: National Digital Sovereignty Framework
consequences: Establishing a national framework for digital sovereignty could centralize control over identity infrastructure but might isolate Denmark from global standards. This lever risks creating technical silos if international alignment is not maintained.
options: Define national standards for platform-neutral authentication infrastructure
Invest in local tech development to replace foreign-dependent components
Create oversight committees to audit supplier concentration risks
review: Centralized control could hinder international collaboration, leaving a gap in European alignment efforts.

name: Citizen-Centric Trust Models
consequences: Shifting focus to user-controlled authentication methods could enhance privacy but may reduce system reliability if adoption is inconsistent. This lever risks underutilization if citizens prioritize convenience over security.
options: Implement user-owned digital credentials with decentralized verification mechanisms
Launch pilot programs to test self-sovereign identity solutions
Educate citizens on the risks of platform-dependent authentication
review: User-centric models may struggle with adoption rates, leaving a gap in critical service accessibility during transition periods.

name: Geopolitical Risk Mitigation Strategies
consequences: Addressing geopolitical risks through diversified supplier relationships could stabilize infrastructure but may increase operational complexity. This lever risks overcomplicating procurement processes if too many alternatives are pursued.
options: Map geopolitical risks associated with foreign platform dependencies
Diversify supplier portfolios to include non-traditional tech providers
Develop contingency plans for sudden policy or market shifts
review: Diversification may create management overhead, leaving a gap in streamlined procurement execution.

name: Decentralized Trust Architecture
consequences: Adopting a blockchain-based identity verification layer reduces reliance on platform-specific signals but introduces complexity in compliance with eIDAS2 and NSIS standards. This shifts technical risk from platform vendors to internal infrastructure management, requiring new governance frameworks for consensus mechanisms.
options: Implement a decentralized identity verification protocol using blockchain technology
Establish a public-private consortium to develop open-source authentication standards
Integrate hardware-based authentication tokens into existing public services
review: Decentralization increases technical complexity and compliance overhead, leaving gaps in interoperability with legacy MitID systems and untested scalability under high transaction volumes.

name: Multi-Platform Authentication Standards
consequences: Creating cross-platform authentication protocols minimizes vendor lock-in but demands coordination across Apple, Google, and alternative OS ecosystems. This risks diluting technical focus, as standardization efforts may prioritize compatibility over innovation in fallback mechanisms.
options: Develop FIDO2/WebAuthn extensions compatible with iOS, Android, and GrapheneOS
Lobby for EU-wide mandates requiring platform-neutral authentication APIs in public services
Pilot browser-based authentication using WebAuthn across municipal digital portals
review: Standardization delays proprietary innovation and creates dependency on fragmented regulatory timelines, leaving critical fallback scenarios unaddressed during transitional periods.

name: Legal Resilience Frameworks
consequences: Enshrining platform-neutral requirements in procurement law forces vendors to adapt but risks litigation from platform providers. This shifts legal exposure from the state to private contractors, potentially slowing implementation through prolonged dispute resolution.
options: Amend public procurement contracts to mandate fallback authentication options for critical services
File regulatory complaints against platform vendors for anti-competitive practices in identity access
Create a legal sandbox for testing platform-neutral authentication models under existing eIDAS2 rules
review: Legal challenges divert resources from technical development and create uncertainty in vendor partnerships, leaving enforcement mechanisms unproven in court.

name: Public-Private Resilience Testing
consequences: Conducting joint stress tests with platform vendors exposes system vulnerabilities but risks compromising proprietary security models. This creates a dependency on vendor cooperation for remediation, which may not align with their business interests.
options: Simulate platform outage scenarios with MitID operators to validate fallback protocols
Establish a shared incident-response framework with Apple and Google for authentication failures
Run parallel authentication trials using alternative platforms alongside live MitID operations
review: Testing exposes operational weaknesses to vendors, creating leverage for them to delay or obstruct implementation while leaving long-term resilience metrics unverified.

name: Citizen-Driven Authentication Trials
consequences: Engaging citizens in beta testing alternative authentication methods builds grassroots support but risks public backlash from usability issues. This shifts responsibility for adoption from institutions to end-users, complicating scalability efforts.
options: Launch a voluntary pilot program for GrapheneOS-based authentication in select municipalities
Create a citizen advisory board to co-design fallback authentication interfaces
Use social media campaigns to recruit beta testers for alternative authentication methods
review: User-centric trials increase political visibility but create dependency on public perception, leaving technical validation incomplete without institutional buy-in.

name: Cross-Border Authentication Partnerships
consequences: Collaborating with Nordic peers strengthens collective bargaining power but dilutes focus on Denmark-specific requirements. This risks aligning with regional standards that may not address local procurement or resilience needs.
options: Form a Nordic consortium to negotiate platform-neutral authentication terms with Apple and Google
Adopt Swedish or Norwegian authentication models as interim fallback solutions
Jointly fund research into cross-border identity verification protocols
review: Regional collaboration fragments technical priorities and creates dependency on external policy timelines, leaving Denmark's unique procurement challenges unaddressed.

name: Vendor-Agnostic Certification Processes
consequences: Revising certification criteria to exclude platform-specific requirements forces vendors to adapt but creates ambiguity in compliance standards. This risks delaying deployment as vendors navigate unclear technical specifications.
options: Revise NSIS certification to exclude platform integrity APIs as mandatory criteria
Create a third-party audit body for platform-neutral authentication solutions
Publish open-source validation tools for alternative authentication methods
review: Certification ambiguity slows vendor adoption and creates legal exposure for institutions, leaving technical verification incomplete without standardized benchmarks.
